---
title: "JSC370 Final Project"
author: "Christopher Li"
jupyter: python3
execute:
  warning: false
  message: false
  echo: false
  output: false
fig-cap-location: bottom
number-sections: true
---

# Two Ratings, One Professor: A Statistical Look at How U of T Professors Are Rated

Every time course selection rolls around, you can spot the furious searching from every student, browsing their rating website of choice to determine which courses, and by extension professors, to choose. The two most common? Rate My Professor and the official Course Evaluations.  

In this project, we compare and contrast these two methods: one official and one personal. We will first do some exploratory analysis on each dataset, exploring how they compare in several key areas, followed by some statistical analysis & modeling. 

```{python}
import pandas as pd
import statsmodels.api as sm
import numpy as np
import json
import plotly.express as px
import plotly.graph_objects as go
import plotly.io as pio
from plotly.subplots import make_subplots

pio.templates.default = "plotly_dark"

import nltk

nltk.download("vader_lexicon")
from nltk.sentiment import SentimentIntensityAnalyzer

from collections import Counter

import statsmodels.api as sm

from pygam import LinearGAM, s
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error, r2_score
from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import CountVectorizer
from sklearn.decomposition import LatentDirichletAllocation
```

```{python}
df_evals = pd.read_csv("../UofT-Course-Data/data/course_evals.csv")
df_rmp = pd.read_csv("../UofT-Course-Data/data/ratemyprof.csv")
df_rmp_ratings = pd.read_csv("../UofT-Course-Data/data/ratings.csv")

with open("../UofT-Course-Data/data/prof_mapping.json") as f:
    prof_mapping = json.load(f)

df_rmp_ratings['date'] = pd.to_datetime(
    df_rmp_ratings['date'].str.replace(r'\s*\+.*$', '', regex=True),
    format='%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S',
    errors='coerce'
)
```

## Datasets

The data used in this analysis comes from two sources: U of T Evaluations and Rate My Professor. The *U of T Course Evaluations** dataset was collected via webscraping and contain a per-section instructor ratings across nine dimensions (INS1–INS6, ARTSC1-ARTSC-3) for courses spanning multiple years. The **RateMyProfessor (RMP)** dataset was scraped from the public RMP website and includes per-rating helpfulness, clarity, and difficulty scores, as well as free-text comments and tag selections left by students voluntarily.

Note that the tables shown below do not represent the full dataset, and should only be used for illustrative purposes. The the dataset descriptions and raw files are available at [github.com/ChristopherLi05/UofT-Course-Data](https://github.com/ChristopherLi05/UofT-Course-Data). 

### U of T Course Evaluations

```{python}
df_evals["average_ins"] = round(
    (df_evals["INS1"] + df_evals["INS2"] + df_evals["INS3"] +
     df_evals["INS4"] + df_evals["INS5"]) / 5, 2
)
df_evals["full_name"] = df_evals["first_name"] + " " + df_evals["last_name"]
```

```{python}
#| output: true

go.Figure(data=[go.Table(
    header=dict(
        values=["Professor", "Course", "Year", "Average Rating"],
        align='left'
    ),
    cells=dict(
        values=[df_evals["full_name"], df_evals["course"], df_evals["year"], df_evals["average_ins"]],
        align='left',
        height=30
    )
)]).show()
```


### Rate My Professor Ratings

```{python}
df_rmp_ratings["year"] = df_rmp_ratings["date"].dt.year
df_rmp["full_name"] = df_rmp["firstName"] + " " + df_rmp["lastName"]

df_rmp_ratings = pd.merge(df_rmp_ratings, df_rmp[["id", "full_name"]], left_on="prof_id", right_on="id").drop(labels=["id"], axis=1)
df_rmp_ratings["average_rating"] = round((df_rmp_ratings["helpfulRating"] + df_rmp_ratings["clarityRating"]) / 2, 2)
```

```{python}
#| output: true
df_temp = df_rmp_ratings.sample(frac=1).reset_index(drop=True)

go.Figure(data=[go.Table(
    header=dict(values=["Professor", "Course", "Year", "Average Rating"], align='left'),
    cells=dict(
        values=[df_temp["full_name"], df_temp["class"], df_temp["year"], df_temp["average_rating"]],
        align='left',
        height=30
    )
)]).show()
```

## Data Exploration

If you look at the original dataset, you may have noticed that the fields do not match. However, as professors are primarily rated based upon the courses that they teach, we can assume that the average of the INS categories are how students will actually rate their professor.

```{python}
#| output: true
#| label: fig-ratings-dist
#| fig-cap: "Distribution of instructor ratings from official course evaluations and RateMyProfessor (RMP). Official evaluations are heavily concentrated near 4.2–4.39, while RMP ratings are much flatter, suggesting that students who voluntarily rate professors on RMP tend to give more critical assessments than those captured in mandatory course evaluations."

fig = go.Figure()

fig.add_trace(go.Histogram(
    x=df_evals.groupby(["first_name", "last_name"])["average_ins"].mean(),
    name="Official Eval",
    xbins=dict(start=1, end=5, size=0.2),
    histnorm="probability",
    opacity=0.75
))

fig.add_trace(go.Histogram(
    x=df_rmp["avgRating"],
    name="RMP Eval",
    xbins=dict(start=1, end=5, size=0.2),
    histnorm="probability",
    opacity=0.75
))

fig.update_layout(
    barmode="overlay",
    title="Distribution of Instructor Ratings: Official Evals vs RateMyProfessor",
    xaxis_title="Rating (1–5)",
    yaxis_title="Proportion",
    legend=dict(x=0.01, y=0.99),
    margin=dict(t=100)
)
fig.show()
```

```{python}
#| output: true
#| label: fig-ins-vs-rmp
#| fig-cap: "Scatter plot of each professor's average official INS score vs their average RMP rating. Each point represents one professor matched across both datasets, with at least 5 ratings in each system. A positive trend is present, indicating that the RMP Rating & INS Score tends to be fairly correlated. This is to be expected."

prof_ins = df_evals.groupby("full_name").filter(lambda x: len(x) >= 5).groupby("full_name")["average_ins"].mean().reset_index()
prof_ins.columns = ["full_name", "avg_ins"]

matched = []
for full_name, rmp_ids in prof_mapping.items():
    rmp_row = df_rmp[(df_rmp["id"].isin(rmp_ids)) & (df_rmp["numRatings"] >= 5)]
    if rmp_row.empty:
        continue
    ins_row = prof_ins[prof_ins["full_name"].str.upper() == full_name.upper()]
    if ins_row.empty:
        continue
    matched.append({
        "full_name": full_name,
        "avg_ins": ins_row["avg_ins"].values[0],
        "avg_rmp": rmp_row["avgRating"].mean()
    })

df_matched = pd.DataFrame(matched)

fig = px.scatter(
    df_matched,
    x="avg_ins",
    y="avg_rmp",
    hover_name="full_name",
    trendline="ols",
    labels={"avg_ins": "Average INS Score", "avg_rmp": "Average RMP Rating"},
    title="Official Eval Score vs RMP Rating by Professor"
)
fig.show()
```

As a baseline comparison, a simple OLS regression was fit predicting average RMP rating from average INS score alone.

| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| R² | 0.348 |
| Adjusted R² | 0.348 |
| F-statistic | 710.7 |
| Prob (F-statistic) | < 0.001 |
| Log Likelihood | -1468.8 |
| AIC | 2942 |
| Number of Observations | 1332 |

| Feature | Coefficient | Std. Error | t | P-value | 95% CI |
|---------|-------------|------------|---|---------|--------|
| Intercept | -2.7958 | 0.242 | -11.542 | < 0.001 | [-3.271, -2.321] |
| avg_ins | 1.5623 | 0.059 | 26.658 | < 0.001 | [1.447, 1.677] |
(Table 1)

The OLS model finds a significant positive relationship between INS score and RMP rating: for every  1-point increase in INS score, RMP rating is predicted to increase by approximately 1.56 points. However, the model only explains 34.8% of variance in RMP ratings, which may be an issue.

```{python}
X = sm.add_constant(df_matched["avg_ins"])
y = df_matched["avg_rmp"]
model = sm.OLS(y, X).fit()

print(model.summary())

df_matched["fitted"] = model.fittedvalues
df_matched["residual"] = model.resid
```

```{python}
#| output: true
#| label: fig-ins-vs-rmp-residual
#| fig-cap: "Residual plot from OLS regression of RMP rating on official INS score. Each point is one professor. The residual plot shows a clear funnel, which violates some OLS assumptions. A more complex model is required."

fig = px.scatter(
    df_matched,
    x="fitted",
    y="residual",
    hover_name="full_name",
    labels={"fitted": "Fitted Values", "residual": "Residuals"},
    title="Residual Plot: RMP Rating ~ INS Score"
)
fig.add_hline(y=0, line_dash="dash", line_color="red")
fig.update_layout(margin=dict(t=100))
fig.show()
```

```{python}
#| output: true
#| label: fig-difficulty-vs-rating
#| fig-cap: "Scatter plot of average difficulty vs average rating on RateMyProfessor, restricted to professors with at least 5 ratings. A negative trend is present, suggesting students rate harder professors lower. This is to be expected."

fig = px.scatter(
    df_rmp[df_rmp["numRatings"] >= 5],
    x="avgDifficulty",
    y="avgRating",
    hover_name="full_name",
    trendline="ols",
    labels={"avgDifficulty": "Average Difficulty (1–5)", "avgRating": "Average Rating (1–5)"},
    title="RMP Difficulty vs Rating by Professor"
)
fig.update_layout(margin=dict(t=100))
fig.show()
```

As a baseline comparison, a simple OLS regression was fit predicting average RMP rating from average difficulty score alone. 

| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| R² | 0.327 |
| Adjusted R² | 0.327 |
| F-statistic | 1339 |
| Prob (F-statistic) | < 0.001 |
| Log Likelihood | -3303.8 |
| AIC | 6612 |
| Number of Observations | 2758 |

| Feature | Coefficient | Std. Error | t | P-value | 95% CI |
|---------|-------------|------------|---|---------|--------|
| Intercept | 6.4447 | 0.080 | 80.773 | < 0.001 | [6.288, 6.601] |
| avgDifficulty | -0.8975 | 0.025 | -36.589 | < 0.001 | [-0.946, -0.849] |
(Table 2)

The OLS model finds a significant negative relationship between difficulty and RMP rating: for every 1-point increase in difficulty, RMP rating is predicted to decrease by approximately 0.90 points. Both the intercept and difficulty coefficient are highly significant (p < 0.001), and the model explains 32.7% 
of variance in RMP ratings.

```{python}
df_rmp_filtered = df_rmp[df_rmp["numRatings"] >= 5].dropna(subset=["avgDifficulty", "avgRating"]).copy()

X = sm.add_constant(df_rmp_filtered["avgDifficulty"])
y = df_rmp_filtered["avgRating"]
model = sm.OLS(y, X).fit()

df_rmp_filtered["fitted"] = model.fittedvalues
df_rmp_filtered["residual"] = model.resid

print(model.summary())
```

```{python}
#| output: true
#| label: fig-difficulty-vs-rating-residual
#| fig-cap: "Residual plot from OLS regression of RMP rating on average difficulty, restricted to professors with at least 5 ratings. The residual plot shows a clear funnel, which violates some OLS assumptions. A more complex model is required."

fig = px.scatter(
    df_rmp_filtered,
    x="fitted",
    y="residual",
    hover_name="full_name",
    labels={"fitted": "Fitted Values", "residual": "Residuals"},
    title="Residual Plot: RMP Rating ~ Difficulty"
)
fig.add_hline(y=0, line_dash="dash", line_color="red")
fig.update_layout(margin=dict(t=100))
fig.show()
```

```{python}
#| output: true
#| label: fig-rmp-tags
#| fig-cap: "Top 20 most frequently used rating tags left by students on RateMyProfessor, restricted to professors with at least 5 ratings. Tags reflect common themes in student experience. There is a mix of both positive and negative tags."

valid_prof_ids = df_rmp[df_rmp["numRatings"] >= 5]["id"].unique()

all_tags = (
    df_rmp_ratings[df_rmp_ratings["prof_id"].isin(valid_prof_ids)]["ratingTags"]
    .dropna()
    .str.split("--")
    .explode()
    .str.strip()
)
all_tags = all_tags[all_tags != ""]

tag_counts = Counter(all_tags).most_common(20)
df_tags = pd.DataFrame(tag_counts, columns=["tag", "count"]).sort_values("count")

fig = px.bar(
    df_tags,
    x="count",
    y="tag",
    orientation="h",
    labels={"count": "Count", "tag": "Tag"},
    title="Top 20 RMP Rating Tags"
)
fig.update_layout(margin=dict(t=100))
fig.show()
```

```{python}
#| output: true
#| label: fig-vader-sentiment
#| fig-cap: "Distribution of VADER compound sentiment scores from RateMyProfessor comments, restricted to professors with at least 5 ratings. Scores range from -1 (most negative) to +1 (most positive). We notice a slight bimodal distribution, with most comments concentrating around very positive. This is fairly interesting to see."

valid_prof_ids = df_rmp[df_rmp["numRatings"] >= 5]["id"].unique()

df_filtered_ratings = df_rmp_ratings[df_rmp_ratings["prof_id"].isin(valid_prof_ids)].copy()

sia = SentimentIntensityAnalyzer()
df_filtered_ratings["sentiment"] = (
    df_filtered_ratings["comment"]
    .dropna()
    .apply(lambda x: sia.polarity_scores(x)["compound"])
)

fig = px.histogram(
    df_filtered_ratings.dropna(subset=["sentiment"]),
    x="sentiment",
    nbins=50,
    labels={"sentiment": "VADER Compound Score", "count": "Count"},
    title="Distribution of RMP Comment Sentiment Scores"
)
fig.update_layout(yaxis_title="Count", margin=dict(t=100))
fig.show()
```

## Data Modeling

### Model Exploration

We previously explored that simple OLS's are not suitable for modeling some of this data. Hence, we try GAMs to see how well they perform. 

```{python}
# Build modeling dataset from matched professors
prof_ins = df_evals.groupby("full_name").filter(lambda x: len(x) >= 5).groupby("full_name")["average_ins"].mean().reset_index()
prof_ins.columns = ["full_name", "avg_ins"]

matched = []
for full_name, rmp_ids in prof_mapping.items():
    rmp_row = df_rmp[(df_rmp["id"].isin(rmp_ids)) & (df_rmp["numRatings"] >= 5)]
    if rmp_row.empty:
        continue
    ins_row = prof_ins[prof_ins["full_name"].str.upper() == full_name.upper()]
    if ins_row.empty:
        continue
    matched.append({
        "full_name": full_name,
        "avg_ins": ins_row["avg_ins"].values[0],
        "avg_rmp": rmp_row["avgRating"].mean(),
        "avgDifficulty": rmp_row["avgDifficulty"].mean()
    })

df_matched = pd.DataFrame(matched).dropna()

feature_cols = ["avg_ins", "avgDifficulty"]
X = df_matched[feature_cols].values
y = df_matched["avg_rmp"].values

X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, test_size=0.2, random_state=370)
```

A multiple OLS regression was fit predicting average RMP rating from average INS score and average difficulty.

| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| R² | 0.510 |
| Adjusted R² | 0.509 |
| F-statistic | 552.4 |
| Prob (F-statistic) | < 0.001 |
| Log Likelihood | -1023.3 |
| AIC | 2053 |
| Number of Observations | 1065 |
| Test RMSE | 0.5738 |
| Test R² | 0.5938 |

| Feature | Coefficient | Std. Error | t | P-value | 95% CI |
|---------|-------------|------------|---|---------|--------|
| Intercept | 0.9579 | 0.293 | 3.265 | < 0.001 | [0.382, 1.534] |
| avg_ins | 1.1709 | 0.059 | 19.732 | < 0.001 | [1.054, 1.287] |
| avgDifficulty | -0.6744 | 0.033 | -20.187 | < 0.001 | [-0.740, -0.609] |
(Table 3)

Both predictors are highly significant (p < 0.001). Average INS score has a positive effect on RMP 
rating: a 1-point increase in INS score is associated with approximately a 1.17-point increase in 
RMP rating. Conversely, avgDifficulty has a negative effect: a 1-point increase in difficulty is 
associated with approximately a 0.67-point decrease in RMP rating. 
A test R² of 0.59 indicates that only about 59% of the data's variance is explained by the model.

```{python}
X_train_sm = sm.add_constant(X_train)
X_test_sm = sm.add_constant(X_test)

ols_model = sm.OLS(y_train, X_train_sm).fit()
print(ols_model.summary())

y_pred = ols_model.predict(X_test_sm)
rmse = np.sqrt(mean_squared_error(y_test, y_pred))
r2 = r2_score(y_test, y_pred)

print(f"Test RMSE: {rmse:.4f}")
print(f"Test R²: {r2:.4f}")
```

```{python}
#| output: true
#| label: fig-ols-residual
#| fig-cap: "Residual plot from OLS regression predicting RMP rating from average INS score and average difficulty. Each point represents one professor in the test set. A clear funnel shape is present, indicating that more complex models are needed."

y_pred = ols_model.predict(X_test_sm)
residuals = y_test - y_pred

fig = px.scatter(
    x=y_pred,
    y=residuals,
    labels={"x": "Fitted Values", "y": "Residuals"},
    title="Residual Plot: OLS (avg_ins + avgDifficulty → avg_rmp)"
)
fig.add_hline(y=0, line_dash="dash", line_color="red")
fig.update_layout(margin=dict(t=100))
fig.show()
```

The GAM model was fit using two smooth terms: average INS score (`s(0)`) and average RMP difficulty (`s(1)`), 
predicting a professor's average RMP rating. Smoothing parameters were selected via grid search cross-validation.

| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| Test RMSE | 0.5766 |
| Test R² | 0.5899 |
| Pseudo R² | 0.5235 |
| Log Likelihood | -1008.322 |
| AIC | 2030.364 |
| GCV | 0.395 |
| Effective DoF | 5.860 |
| Number of Samples | 1065 |

| Feature | Lambda | EDoF | P-value | Significance |
|---------|--------|------|---------|--------------|
| avg_ins | 1000 | 3.4 | < 0.001 | *** |
| avgDifficulty | 1000 | 2.5 | < 0.001 | *** |
| Intercept | — | 0.0 | < 0.001 | *** |
(Table 4)

Both smooth terms are highly significant (p < 0.001), indicating that both the official INS score and 
RMP difficulty contribute meaningfully to predicting RMP ratings. The effective degrees of freedom (EDoF) 
greater than 1 for both terms confirms that the relationships are nonlinear, justifying the use of a GAM 
over a simpler linear model. The model explains approximately 59% of variance on the test set (R² = 0.59).
We achieve smilar R², but the violation of assumptions imply that this is the correct model to use. 

```{python}
gam = LinearGAM(s(0) + s(1)).gridsearch(X_train, y_train)

y_pred = gam.predict(X_test)
rmse = np.sqrt(mean_squared_error(y_test, y_pred))
r2 = r2_score(y_test, y_pred)

print(f"Test RMSE: {rmse:.4f}")
print(f"Test R²: {r2:.4f}")
print(gam.summary())
```

```{python}
#| output: true
#| label: fig-gam-result
#| fig-cap: "GAM partial dependence plots predicting RMP average rating from official INS score and average difficulty, for professors with at least 5 ratings in each system. Shaded regions represent 95% confidence intervals. We fit a singular GAM with the two predictors we are interested in. This follows a similar pattern as earler, where there is a positive correlation with average INS score, and a negative correlation with difficulty."

# Partial dependence plots
feature_labels = ["Average INS Score", "Average Difficulty"]

fig = make_subplots(rows=1, cols=len(feature_cols), subplot_titles=feature_labels)

for i, (col, label) in enumerate(zip(feature_cols, feature_labels)):
    XX = gam.generate_X_grid(term=i)
    pdep, confi = gam.partial_dependence(term=i, X=XX, width=0.95)
    x_vals = XX[:, i]

    fig.add_trace(
        go.Scatter(x=x_vals, y=pdep, mode="lines", name=label, line=dict(color="steelblue")),
        row=1, col=i+1
    )
    fig.add_trace(
        go.Scatter(
            x=np.concatenate([x_vals, x_vals[::-1]]),
            y=np.concatenate([confi[:, 0], confi[:, 1][::-1]]),
            fill="toself",
            fillcolor="rgba(70, 130, 180, 0.3)",
            line=dict(color="rgba(255,255,255,0)"),
            showlegend=False,
            name=f"{label} CI"
        ),
        row=1, col=i+1
    )
    fig.add_hline(y=0, line_dash="dash", line_color="red", row=1, col=i+1)
    fig.update_xaxes(title_text=label, row=1, col=i+1)
    fig.update_yaxes(title_text="Partial Effect", row=1, col=i+1)

fig.update_layout(title="GAM Partial Dependence Plots", showlegend=False,
    margin=dict(t=100))
fig.show()
```

### LDA Topic Modeling

Next, we explore some common words used in the RMP ratings.

```{python}
valid_prof_ids = df_rmp[df_rmp["numRatings"] >= 5]["id"].unique()

comments = (
    df_rmp_ratings[df_rmp_ratings["prof_id"].isin(valid_prof_ids)]["comment"]
    .dropna()
    .str.lower()
    .str.replace(r"[^a-z\s]", "", regex=True)
    .str.strip()
)
comments = comments[comments != ""]

vectorizer = CountVectorizer(
    max_df=0.95,
    min_df=5,
    stop_words="english",
    max_features=1000
)
dtm = vectorizer.fit_transform(comments)

n_topics = 5
lda = LatentDirichletAllocation(n_components=n_topics, random_state=370, max_iter=20)
lda.fit(dtm)

# Get top words per topic
n_top_words = 10
vocab = vectorizer.get_feature_names_out()

topics = []
for topic_idx, topic in enumerate(lda.components_):
    top_words = [vocab[i] for i in topic.argsort()[:-n_top_words - 1:-1]]
    top_weights = sorted(topic, reverse=True)[:n_top_words]
    for word, weight in zip(top_words, top_weights):
        topics.append({"topic": f"Topic {topic_idx + 1}", "word": word, "weight": weight})

df_topics = pd.DataFrame(topics)
```

```{python}
#| output: true
#| label: fig-lda
#| fig-cap: "LDA of the most common word per category"

topic_descriptions = [
    "Workload & Assignments",
    "Positive Reviews",
    "Teaching Quality",
    "Personality Reviews",
    "Negative Reviews"
]

fig = make_subplots(rows=1, cols=n_topics, subplot_titles=[f"Topic {i+1}<br>{topic_descriptions[i]}" for i in range(n_topics)])
fig.update_annotations(font_size=10)

for i in range(n_topics):
    df_t = df_topics[df_topics["topic"] == f"Topic {i+1}"].sort_values("weight")
    fig.add_trace(
        go.Bar(x=df_t["weight"], y=df_t["word"], orientation="h", showlegend=False),
        row=1, col=i+1
    )
    fig.update_xaxes(title_text="Weight", row=1, col=i+1)

fig.update_layout(
    title="LDA Topic Modeling: Top Words per Topic",
    height=500,
    margin=dict(t=100)
)
fig.show()
```
